speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Mabel Oliver Hazeltine grew up near Viola. On this tape she speaks of her childhood, dances, revivals, toboggan parties, and chores. She recalls the 7th to Advent as a prediction, the 1910 fire was the end of the world, the tent meetings, and going to church in Viola as a child. She also remembers her life in Canada and childbirth.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: They're going over parks that come out any of these. And if he caught them in between, the only place they were safe was right in the end, in those places there. And if he caught them, come out here and caught them in between those places, then whoever caught them, they had to be the fox.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were those pathways in the snow?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Uh -huh. Yeah, we'd go out in the snow and try and find a big circle.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: And then the lanes and...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: what was that called again?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Box and Goose.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: You were the geese out on the circle. That was our main, that and ice skating. It was our main winter games outside.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Where could you ice skate?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Oh, I went to school out at Four Mile, down below Viola. They used to have a big school down there. At noon, instead of having recess lots of times, in the wintertime when the crickets froze over, the teacher let us kids skip our recesses, and we'd have an hour, hour and a quarter, noon. And we'd take half our lunches, roll them up in wax paper or something in our pockets, and skate from the Schoolhouse corrupt of Viola, or up to where that trussler used to be, you know, the highway there. And back at noon. Well, yeah, there were some pretty good skaters. One of them, there's couple boys out there, could write their names on the ice. Just, you know, without stopping, just skating along, write their names. One of them was Lester Gutchess. He's dead now. And Lloyd Parker, I think, is still alive.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: But...

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: And then we'd play these in -guide games inside the Schoolhouse, as well as when we went to parties. Of course, we always, we usually had it with an organ in the room. Somebody would play the organ, or they'd sing.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: And instead of walking around or running around in these games, we'd dance, see, instead of... In that way, the teacher or any of the parents that didn't leave and dance, and if they heard us singing and all, they figured we'd just play in this game. It was very easy to stop and, you know, if we heard the door open, somebody usually watched the door pretty close. Then we got one teacher that she believed in dancing, so she'd let us dance. She'd watch the door for us, or watch the windows if any of the parents were the captain. But they'd find me some of the kids that was, you know, closer to their parents and whatnot. They squawked on us and they kept us snoopin'. She lost her job.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Did you ice skate must be signs at school, or were there... Oh, yeah.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you go on an evening in ice skating?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Oh, yeah. We went in evenings and then we had toboggan parties. We usually gang up up at our place because we had a great big hill. It'd come straight down, and then... Oh, it'd come down for, I guess, pretty near a mile, three -quarters of a mile, anyway, down the hill. And then there was a cut in the road that went... It's wider now, but at that time it was just one way, what you would call one -way traffic. And we'd get going the past. A lot of times we'd jump there over that road and on to the other guy's feet or cross the road. One time a whole bunch of us went up there skating and... Oh, he was one smart, elite kid that always thought he was smart and he stuck his foot in the snow. The snow had melted and then froze over. It was all icy on top. And, boy, we all went scootin' off. When he'd done that, it turned the toboggan. And, boy, we went to school the next day with burned faces where we... Oh, gosh, I tell you, it was awful. It was sore. And they never let him die at the toboggan after that. Then we'd always usually go back to my house or my folks' place and have a big chili feed. Either that or else pop -pop or corn or pulled happy. Usually I all ended up at my folks '...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you sled much, too?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Oh, yeah. We'd go coasting, too. But usually on these parties, there was always some of them that... Oh, they'd be real parties. I mean, you'd invite people to come over. Well, just usually most of the school kids would get together. And the older ones would go on the toboggan. And some of the smaller ones, maybe, would have their sleds. Because we had an old horse and a big old bobsled that... Well, I had teen horses. And they'd pull the kids and their sleds and toboggan and stuff back up the top of the hill. Otherwise, why, it took us so long, we'd only got about one trip down.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: We had a road that broke around that didn't take only maybe 10 minutes or maybe 15 to go up there. And somebody would always take care of the team and drive it back down. We'd be there to take them back up.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Those were the good old days. The kids don't do those things anymore. All they think about doing is getting out in a darn car and seeing how many people run in the ditch.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Did you...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would you go to dances as a kid? Or was it only when we got older?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Well, we went to a few dances when I was a kid if we could get away with it. Some of the parents believed in dancing. And they'd kind of keep it quiet if they were going to have a dance. So they'd reach out and they'd just tell the kids that they knew they wanted to come to the dances. And of course, the folks got wise to that, too.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: They wouldn't let you go over to certain people's heads if they were crying?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Well, no. When they gave a party, why, they... Well, they just kind of put the kinks in it and stopped at that fall because... The kids wanted to dance, and if they couldn't dance, why, they wouldn't go that fall. And the people that were having them, why, they didn't want to get the end of the other neighbors, so they just quit having them. And after, of course, after we got older, well... We went regardless. To town or people's houses, they had dances, a lot of dances, just around the neighborhood.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was the dance hall at Viola when you were young?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Yeah.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you go there much when you got there?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: I think I only went there a couple of times.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: When you say you went into town, does that mean Moscow?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Moscow or... please, there's somewhere. Mostly Moscow.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Where did you go there?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Well, they... I can't remember who it was. I believe it was the moose. I had a hall up over what is now. I can't remember what the name of that drugstore is there. It's up in the block where David's is. There's a jewelry store down underneath, a drugstore. Well, upstairs used to be a great big hall. I don't know what's up there now. And the moose had that. Well, before I was married, and asked for quite a while after I was married, and then they, I think, finally built their own building, and the Eagles and Lodge had that for a while. And we had dances up there. Of course, that was after I was married.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: We went up there for moose dances, but mostly it was out around country houses and farms. They'd take up their rugs in their front room. Most of their homes had archways between the front room and dining room. We moved everything out, so we had two big rooms to dance in. Then in Canada, we had dances at the schoolhouses. We would stay all night if I'm not there. If you lived very far away, you'd leave maybe 5 -36 o 'clock. Because you had to drive with the team in the wintertime up there, and the snow was deep and cold. Stayed up 6 -8 o 'clock in the morning.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was it a matter of religion that some of the people didn't?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Uh -huh. Uh -huh.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Yeah, there's still some of the religions that doesn't believe in dancing. The religion that my folks belonged to, I don't know exactly what they called it. They just didn't have any name for it.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: I don't know if you know Dorothy Holland or not, do you?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: I haven't met her. You have to be Dorothy Cheney. No. I guess she's moved back up above Viola somewhere now. I see her address in the phone book. The difference used to live down here. Her folks, when she still belongs to the same religion, and they just absolutely don't breathe in shows or dancing or anything like that.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: That was a good story you told me last time about that book you were going to buy for your mother. It was Mary's murder case, I believe it was. Some murder case anyway by Richard Van Dyne. I'll read that by the guy that wrote it.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: No.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: She wouldn't think of going into a show. Buy the book and read it.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Yeah, we think it works.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were they connected with the evangelists, Tamp and Viola?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Now, those were the ones at the best, but they, when the folks got joined in with them when they came there. Had their meetings in a big tent.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you ever go to their meetings?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you remember much about it?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Not too much. I remember more about one that had a big, I guess they'd call it, probably call it a district meeting or whatever, what they called it now. But it was all different members from way around here, and it was up at Hayden Lake. And I remember when I was just a kid, we went to Coeur d'Alene on the train, and we took a boat from there to Harrison.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: And then we, it wasn't right in Harrison, it was at some farm that just built a new barn. And, gosh, I tell you, I don't know, there must have been thousands of people there.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Some of them brought a half a beef, and everybody brought food. And you had to bring your own bedding, too. Some of them brought potatoes, some of them brought this, and some of them brought that. My gosh, we ate like kings. But I was only a kid then, about, oh, 12, 14, I guess. And I didn't go to many of their meetings. I and a bunch of the other kids was usually out rambling around through the trees and woods and down the lake swimming. And they were trying to swim. I couldn't swim, but I'd get in there and wait around.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How long did that last?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: It lasted for, I don't know, 10 days, 2 weeks. Well, we were up there at least a week.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Well, I remember, within the summertime, there was just a while before harvest, because the folks I know had to get home for harvest, or you got to go for harvest.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: But that's about all I remember too much about the meetings.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: There was a thing where they wouldn't accept money, right?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Mm -hmm. From people. Unless you were a member. They wouldn't. And outside, they wouldn't take a penny from them. They never passed the hat around or anything like that, or passed the plate.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: When the Seventh -day Adventist came in, did they hold meetings in town?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Oh, yeah. I don't know if they had them in tents. I don't remember. All I remember about the Seventh -day Adventist was, I was only about, I don't know, 8, 9 or something. Like, that wasn't very big. And folks had always insisted on us going to Sunday school. They'd always, according to the Bible, the world wouldn't be destroyed by rain or no floods no more. The only other way I could think of was fire. Then when these mountains got on fire up there, they started spreading it around. The world was coming to an end. I'd go to bed at night and you'd think I could sleep, trying to think of some way I could get away from where that fire was going to come. They just scared the stuffing out of me because I just knew that that was the only way that the world could be destroyed with a fire or a flood. And the rainbow was supposed to be a sign that would never flood again.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were all the kids scared of that?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Well, quite a few of them were.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were your parents, did they believe in that?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: No.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: They didn't believe in it.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: But you know, you take a kid and they try to figure things out, and the only way they can figure them out was...

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: And that is a lot of the way that they got a lot of their members because they just scared them into, you know, so they'd be saved. The only ones that were going to be saved was the Adventists.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: They didn't lose a lot of their converts once the fires didn't come?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: I don't think so. They used to have quite a big settlement out there by old line. And I guess there's still some quite a few of them out there.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: And a lot... Were most of those people, were they converts from the area to the church, or were they from...

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: had they moved in to the... I don't know. I don't know what they did about that. I know they built a school up there, and they built a church up above Iowa up in there. And it was just practically all quite a settlement of Adventists up there. And a lot of their beliefs is true. I agree with them on a lot of things. A lot of wonderful people that are Adventists. There's Eddie Gray out there at Violets, and gee, I don't think there's any nicer person anywhere than his family are.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: But there's some of the things that I don't agree on. I think the devil's going to get you to eat pork. And I have read the Bible enough that I know it. It says in there, it's not what goes into the mouth, it's what comes out.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you go into Violets?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Oh yeah, quite a bit. We used to go up there to literary, and... That's where the folks went to church, always, up there, at Violets, and before these guys came in in the tents, they went to the church up there. And we went to Sunday school.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: And on Sunday, after church, I... Practically the whole congregation, or two -thirds of them, or a third of them at least, anyway, gathered up at somebody's house for a big dinner. First one, then the other one you know around.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would you dress up for church?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Oh yeah. We wore our Sunday best when we went to church.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: I remember one time my mother getting off from at me because we always had our school dresses and our Sunday dress.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: And I wanted... I got a new school dress, and I wanted to wear that to church. No, my father told me, put on my Sunday dress.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: I said, you mean my damn church dress?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Ah! I wasn't going to call it a Sunday dress, I wasn't going to call it a church dress.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would people bring food, or would one family just feed everyone that came?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: No, just one family, because you never knew where you were going. You'd get there, and they'd walk them on up to our house for dinner.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would your kids be able to play?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Oh yeah, we'd go outside and play inside. Practically all the houses had big... farms had big houses.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: There was very few houses in Viola at that time. Most of them were just farms around Viola.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would you ever go to town after school to fool around or anything?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: I don't know.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: When I was a kid at home, until I started dating and running around, if I got to town twice a year, I'd done well. Folks, they didn't go to town. They didn't used to go to town like they do now. Dad'd go to town in the spring and...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You mean Moscow or Palouse?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: He'd go to town and bring home all the staple stuff that we didn't raise on the farm. There was nothing bought in town besides coffee and sugar and stuff like that. Raisins and walnuts to have to cook with. He always mixed our own baking powder. He'd get the soda and the prima tartar and mix it himself. Our flour, we made that ourself, our whole wheat flour.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You'd grind it yourself?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: The whole wheat flour he'd come down maybe and get, I don't know, 15, 20, 50 pound sacks of flour in the spring, white flour. Of course, in our own meat, we always raised that. Vegetables, all that stuff was canned on fruit. Rice and coffee and sugar and white flour and raisins and nuts and stuff to bake with and cook with. Come down with the wagon and team and the spring and in the fall. I can remember getting maybe 300 pound sacks of sugar at a time. Maybe more in the spring than I have for sugar for canning and whatnot.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: We always raised all of our own meat.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you can many vegetables or was it managed as fruit?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: No, no. It was vegetables and all. They were like peas and corn and string beans and stuff that wouldn't keep. That was canned. But our potatoes and carrots, we had big bins in an underground cellar. Then dad had sand and I don't know different stuff that would keep them. They would keep. I remember our onions and cabbage and stuff was usually put up. We had a great big barn and dad would usually leave a place right in the middle of the hayloft. A great big square place. He'd have hay underneath it and our squash and pumpkin and onions. I don't know what all stuff that would keep there was put in there. Then it was covered over the top with hay and there was an alley way to go back in there to get it. That wouldn't freeze it. Uh -uh. Not in there with hay. Of course the horses, it was all down underneath. They kept it, the bottom of it, fairly warm. Then it was covered up above with hay. He had a frame built so the hay couldn't drop down in there. Of course our carrots and rutabiggis and parsnips and all that stuff. Well the parsnips were usually left in the ground. Sometimes the carrots too because it didn't hurt them. The cabbage and onions and pumpkins and squash and whatnot was put up in there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you have any chores that you had to do?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: You got it. We weren't told about them either. That is we were told when we got old enough and come our time. When I came home from school, my first thing that I did was stay on my horse and go up and bring down the milk cows and stuff off of the big pasture and get them in the crayon. Put my horse in the barn and come to the house and see that there was wood and kindling carried in. It was always cut. All I had to do was carry it in and it better be in. Then help get supper and do the dishes.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Dad kept it busy. Dad would come in the house and if that kindling box wasn't full and the wood box wasn't full he wouldn't say one word. He got the strap. You knew darn well he told you once what you were supposed to do and the minute you saw him had come to the house you knew what was going to happen if he didn't have it in. You better have it in. I used to think he was mean but he wasn't. There was no darn sense in having to tell a 9, 10 year old kid every night it's time to get your kindling in, it's time to get your wood in, it's time to do this. You knew you had it to do, you better get it done. I think about that now a lot with my great grandkids and oh God.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Where did your parents come from? To Viola?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: To Viola they came from down on what they called Spring Flat, Missouri Flat or some place it's off down in around Binge, Washington and down in through there somewhere. That's where my oldest sister, that's where they led when they were first married was I think at Binge, Washington. My oldest sister and oldest brother were born there and then they moved from there up... I don't really know. They moved up there to Viola in spring of 1901 just before I was born. I don't know where my next two brothers were born, they were born somewhere after they left Binge because Dale and Ralph were both born at Binge I'm sure. But for Arlie and Charlie I don't know, they were born somewhere on what they called Rebel Flat, Spring Flat, Missouri Flat, some of those flats down there. Because I know, I think it's when they lived at Binge that Spring, Washington was the closest place where they had to go to get groceries and what not and it'd take them all day to make the trip with the team. They'd get up and go real early in the morning I guess just from hearing them talk about it and be real late at night when they got home.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were they homesteading up there?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: I don't know if they did or not before my time so I don't really know but I kind of think they did.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Then they bought the place down at Viola. They bought the place there at Viola.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: A fellow by the name of Angel. Then they bought the place back of him. I fell on that with... I can't think of his name.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Now when did your father go to Canada to get land?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What made him go?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Oh, just... Her property was cheap up there and it was a big opportunity and what not. They went up there and bought... One, two, three sections of land, I believe it was. Two and a half or three sections of land.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did anyone take care of the home place? Out here?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Yeah. After the first year he went up there and bought this. Then he rented it.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did the whole family go up there?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: No.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did your mom?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: My mother didn't go until... The first trip she made up there was in...

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: 1920, I believe. 20 or 21.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: She went up.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: We were down here then. She went up there. Her and my youngest sister got married. Her and her husband and mother went up that year. Then the next year we went back up.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What made you go? Well, the last time or the first time?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: The first time. When you were about...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: how old were you? 15?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: My oldest brother and his wife were going up. Dad had bought a place for them. They were going up to farm. She was expecting a baby and I went up to cook and helped take care of the...

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: one she wasn't able to. To help her that summer.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What did you think of it up there?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Well, I...

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: I was too busy to even think. When you cook for 14, 15, maybe 20 men... you don't have much time to think much.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: For those hired men that they...

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Yeah, almost everywhere. I went from one place from one brother's house to the other brother's house to my dad's house. Cooking, you know, when they were working on these different places.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Then I got married that summer.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: That first summer?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Uh -huh.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do they farm differently up there?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Well, uh... I don't know how they farmed now, but at that time they farmed about the same as they do here. Except up there, there was... at that time there was no fall crops. Everything was planted in the spring. Because it would freeze out in the wintertime. Got too cold. And everything was... spring crops. So therefore they were... spring was a busy time.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were the people up there any different than down here?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Well, some of the Canadians were, but in the settlement where we were, they were an awful, awful lot of... of people from down here they'd all went up there. A lot of them, too. And there's one woman that lives down here on Asbury. Her husband's dead now.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: They were...

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: they went up there. They were real close friends of ours. And it was... well, there was about three families that we knew. Thirty night. Go home sometime Sunday night. Sunday evening. The board got dark.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Now, a lot of... there was some Russians up there and a lot of English people and some French people. I know there's a Russian family that lived just... Well, we could... from out on our front yard, we could see their house and part of their land around their house. And this woman was expecting a baby. And when the neighbors came that afternoon, we'd seen her out there and working in her garden. It was long in May or June. When we come in or when we ride at noon... And then after noon, we saw her out there, too, again. One... about two, three o 'clock. About four o 'clock when the neighbor ladies came up and said, Mrs. So -and -so had her baby, Isaac Wann. Well, about noon, out of my gosh, she was out working in the garden. I says, till noon. And she was out there again around one thirty -two o 'clock. Well, that's the way the Russians did it. And sure enough, she had had her baby and she was out there working. It didn't stop her.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Did you... I've heard that a lot of the women in the old days, they used to lie in bed for ten days. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Oh, yes. I didn't even want you to turn over.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Move around, just lay as still as you could. Till everything went back into place, they figured. Now that's the other way, they want to get you up and move you around.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Well, did she just lie there for ten days, huh?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Well, I never did.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Oh, you didn't?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: No.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: After my second one was born, I was up the third day, giving her bath and whatnot.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: But you just couldn't stay in bed?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Well, that and we couldn't afford to hire a nurse. And my sister -in -law had came up at the time she was... right shortly after she was born. She didn't get there before. And when her kids got sick and she had to go home, so... I couldn't see why I couldn't do it. I didn't think it hurt me and I guess it didn't, I don't know. I lived through it anyway.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you have a doctor come at all up there? Did they have many doctors?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Oh, yeah. They had doctors.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: But the doctor didn't get there when my second one was born until... She was born about midnight and he didn't get there until about four or five o 'clock in the morning. It was a regular blizzard. It was in February and oh, brother, it was a nasty night.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: I sat up in bed and tied the cord myself.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You yourself?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Yep. I did.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: My husband brought me the string and the scissors and I tied the cord, cut it. Rolled her up in a blanket. Had her there in bed beside me when... One time my sister -in -law got there to take care of me. I thought, oh boy, I'm going to have some fun now because she'd had, I don't know, I think five or six kids. And every darn one of them she'd call the doctor two or three times just a false alarm.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: She'd come in and look in the bedroom and she said, you all right?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: And I said, yeah, I'm all right. She sat down out by the stove and getting warm and whatnot and pretty soon she says, I think you're having a false alarm. I might as well go home. Turned the covers down and she got out of bed. There was the baby laying there. And the doctor got there about six o 'clock the next morning. But oh, there's a regular blizzard. It was terrible that night. About 60 below. The wind blowing and the snow blowing.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: That's incredible. In the winter time there was a lake between our place and the doctors, or the town. And it was 14 miles from town. And in the winter time they come across this lake. And when you got to the middle of that lake and everything looked the same and with the snow blowing and drifting and everything, I guess he went around and around and around that lake until practically daylight before he could get headed the right way. Six, seven o 'clock when he got out there the next morning. Then he bumped into one of the neighbors, I guess, or somebody that was going someplace and they got him steered out right.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What made you move back down here?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Oh, I don't know. We just got in our head and wanted to come back down here and farm.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: The kids, the oldest little girl, was getting old enough to go to school. And of course they had the school in the summertime up there. It usually starts, school usually starts or dead. Now they don't have no country schools up where we live. The kids all have to go to town. And they have school buses, but at that time they had no school buses. And the kids, the little kids just simply couldn't go in the wintertime, that was all. School usually started and the last part of March or April depended on the weather. The weather cleared up and started a little earlier in March, but it was always over. But the last of October there was no more school.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: And our kids, our kids would have had about three miles to walk to school and I couldn't see that. And my husband, he wanted to come back down here and farm, so one of his half -brothers came up and sold the farm up there to him.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you like school when you were a kid?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Yeah, pretty good.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Until the last year that I went to school when I was in 11th grade, they taught, see they taught the first two years of high school out there at Four Mile. The first year was fine, the last year we got a teacher that was lazy and she wanted the other kids to, well she kept us busy here in the lower classes. She'd sit up there and read a book and this and that and the other. Finally I just quit. She wasn't teaching us anything. I don't think she could teach high school really. I don't think she knew any more than we did because you'd go to her with some algebra problem or something and maybe she'd get it right and maybe she wouldn't. Was high school very different than - Oh yeah, it was a lot different than it is now. My folks didn't believe in girls, especially if you come into town to go to high school, so that ended it.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What was going to happen in town? What was wrong with a girl?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Well, I just would have nobody to tell them what they could do and what they couldn't do, I guess. I don't know what their idea was. Well, there was a couple of other girls from out there that had come into town here. One of them went to Blue's and one to Moscow and both of them got into trouble.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: And besides the older people, at them days they didn't, you know, they thought, well, a woman is going to be nothing but a housewife. She don't need an education anyway. As long as she can read and write, figurative, that's all that's next there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did that happen often that girls got into trouble?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Not as often then as it did now, but it was a terrible crime then when they did. Now, you know, they don't think nothing about it. But at that time, why, gee, they were just - why, you wasn't supposed to even be seen in the same crowd with them or anything else. They were just terrible, that was all.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did they usually leave the country or something?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Well, sometimes they did, not always. I know there was one girl that went to our school. I heard younger sister and I chummed around a lot together. But after Lizzie got in trouble, I - I - Eddie could come up to our place, but I couldn't go to her place. Lizzie finally got married and moved away.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: That ended up - you ended up having a fight over that, I remember, with your mom.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Can you tell me that one again?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Oh, gosh, I forgot now, just what the heck that was all about. It was about dancing and the fact that Lizzie got pregnant. Well, I figured she got pregnant coming home from a dance.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: I remember you - your mom didn't want you to go to dances because she thought you'd get in trouble.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Yeah, I - I told her if I wanted to get in trouble, I'd get in trouble just as well coming home from Sunday school as I came from the dance.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Oh, yeah, were your parents against music at all?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Oh, no, no, they weren't against music. They didn't care about this old dance music and stuff like that. They liked more of the old songs, like my old Kentucky Home and, you know. And I suppose their idea of Finn was the same as maybe ours is now with this darn rock and roll and whatnot, you know. To me, there's no music to that. It's just a bunch of hollerin' and blairin' and a bunch of noise.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Oh, yeah, there was something about - you told me the story last time about the first drunk man you ever saw.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: I just thought it was workin' for the folks. We got up one Sunday morning, and he was laying out in the front yard. The folks wanted us to go call him for breakfast. None of us would go, but we'd get around and peek around the corners to watch him because we'd never been that close to a drunk man before. One of our neighbors had three boys, and their father drank a lot, and they used to go by on the wagon and get beer or something by the barrel or something. No, they hauled water. That was it. And these barrels, they didn't - their well went dry. I remember my dad was sayin' the way they was rollin' around on the wagon, you didn't know which was the barrel and which is one of the guys. There - every one of them are dead now.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Oh, yeah, when you came back from Canada, were things changed much from when you left?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: Well, not an awful lot, except that a lot of the kids I went to school with and friends and people had moved away and outside of the other conditions, they were about the same. Or we wouldn't have noticed it because we would come down practically every winter from up there and was down here for two, three months. We'd usually come down in last November and stay until February or March and go back up. So, if they changed, we just didn't notice it.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was the railroad being built when you were down here? The inland through Viola?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: That was already built for where we ever left here, I think. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: I don't remember just exactly when it was built, but it was there from the time - Oh, I must have been eight, nine years old anyway, maybe.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you fool around down there when they were building it at all?

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: No, no.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: We were - oh, a good mile from where the railroad went through where we lived. The only time we were ever up there, we'd just cross it to go to Viola or something like that. No.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: I don't even remember. I can just faintly remember when they were building that trestle because I didn't see how they were going to get it up that high as it was, hold a train. But that's about all I can remember about it.

speaker: Mabel Hazeltine
words: He could probably tell you more of the old -time stuff than I can. I don't know about an old -timer. I know.
